---
title: "Wavelets and Multiscale Representation"
subtitle: "MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging - Week 11"
author: "Laurent Najman"
date: "Week 11"
format:
  revealjs:
    incremental: false
execute:
  enabled: false
---

## Opening Question {.inverse-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
Where is the detail?
:::

Fourier tells us which frequencies are present.

How can we also know where they occur?

## Today

::: {.checklist}
- Explain wavelets as localized multiscale patterns.
- Read a 2D wavelet decomposition.
- Connect wavelet coefficients to sparsity.
- Use thresholding for compression and denoising.
- Compare wavelet denoising with ordinary smoothing.
:::

## 75-Minute Plan

| Time | Work |
|---:|---|
| 0-8 min | recap sparse reconstruction |
| 8-22 min | wavelet intuition and Haar atoms |
| 22-36 min | multiscale decomposition |
| 36-48 min | 2D wavelet subbands and sparsity |
| 48-60 min | thresholding, compression, denoising |
| 60-70 min | notebook activity |
| 70-75 min | synthesis and exit check |

## Bridge from Week 10

Week 10 said:

::: {.takeaway-box}
Sparse reconstruction depends on choosing a representation where the image has few important coefficients.
:::

::: {.question-box}
What representation should we choose for localized edges and details?
:::

## Fourier Is Global

A Fourier coefficient describes a global oscillation.

That is powerful for convolution and frequency filtering.

::: {.model-box}
But a local edge affects many Fourier coefficients.
:::

## Wavelets Are Local and Multiscale

Wavelets are patterns that can be:

::: {.checklist}
- shifted in position;
- scaled in size;
- used to describe coarse and fine image structures.
:::

## Part 1: Haar Intuition {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
The simplest wavelet
:::

Average and difference

## Haar Scaling and Wavelet Functions

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week11-haar-atoms.png){fig-alt="Haar scaling function, Haar wavelet, and a shifted scaled Haar wavelet"}
:::

## Average and Detail

For two neighboring values $a$ and $b$:

$$
\text{average}=\frac{a+b}{2},
\qquad
\text{detail}=\frac{a-b}{2}.
$$

::: {.takeaway-box}
The average stores coarse information; the detail stores local change.
:::

## Repeat at Coarser Scales

Apply the same idea to averages:

::: {.checklist}
- first level: small local details;
- second level: larger-scale details;
- third level: even coarser structure.
:::

## Activity 1: Manual Haar Step

::: {.time-tag}
5 minutes
:::

::: {.exercise-box}
For the signal

$$
[8,\ 6,\ 2,\ 2],
$$

compute pairwise averages and details.

Which pair contains a local change?
:::

## Part 2: Multiscale Decomposition {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
Coarse plus details
:::

One signal, several scales

## 1D Multiscale View

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week11-1d-multiscale.png){fig-alt="A 1D signal decomposed into coarse approximation and several wavelet detail scales"}
:::

## Reading the 1D Decomposition

::: {.checklist}
- The approximation contains the broad trend.
- Coarse details capture large jumps.
- Fine details capture narrow changes and oscillations.
- Adding all components reconstructs the signal.
:::

## Energy Is Redistributed

The transform does not create new information.

It reorganizes information into:

$$
\text{coarse approximation}+\text{details at several scales}.
$$

::: {.caption}
For orthogonal wavelets, energy is preserved up to normalization.
:::

## Part 3: Images and Subbands {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
Horizontal, vertical, diagonal
:::

2D wavelets

## 2D Wavelet Transform

For images, the transform is applied along rows and columns.

At each scale, we get:

::: {.checklist}
- approximation coefficients;
- horizontal detail coefficients;
- vertical detail coefficients;
- diagonal detail coefficients.
:::

## Wavelet Coefficient Layout

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week11-image-subbands.png){fig-alt="Image patch and 2-level wavelet coefficient layout"}
:::

## Subband Interpretation

| Subband | Rough meaning |
|---|---|
| LL | coarse approximation |
| LH | horizontal-type detail |
| HL | vertical-type detail |
| HH | diagonal or corner-like detail |

::: {.caption}
Naming conventions vary by library, but the idea is directional detail.
:::

## Activity 2: Locate the Edge

::: {.time-tag}
5 minutes
:::

::: {.exercise-box}
If an image has a strong vertical edge, which detail subband should respond strongly?

Explain in terms of row and column changes.
:::

## Part 4: Sparsity in Wavelet Domain {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
Few large coefficients
:::

Localized detail is economical

## Coefficient Decay

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week11-coefficient-decay.png){fig-alt="Sorted coefficient magnitudes for pixels, DCT, and wavelet representations"}
:::

## Why Wavelets Can Be Sparse

Many image regions are locally smooth.

Only edges and texture create large detail coefficients.

::: {.takeaway-box}
Wavelets often represent piecewise-smooth images with many small coefficients and a few large ones.
:::

## Compression by Keeping Large Coefficients

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week11-wavelet-compression.png){fig-alt="Original image and wavelet reconstructions keeping different fractions of largest coefficients"}
:::

## Hard Thresholding

Hard thresholding keeps a coefficient only if it is large enough:

$$
H_T(c)=
\begin{cases}
c, & |c|\geq T,\\
0, & |c|<T.
\end{cases}
$$

::: {.caption}
This is useful for compression, but it can create artifacts.
:::

## Soft Thresholding

Soft thresholding shrinks coefficients:

$$
S_T(c)=\operatorname{sign}(c)\max(|c|-T,0).
$$

::: {.takeaway-box}
Soft thresholding is the proximal operator behind l1 regularization.
:::

## Part 5: Wavelet Denoising {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
Small details may be noise
:::

Threshold noisy coefficients

## Denoising Idea

Noise often creates many small detail coefficients.

Wavelet denoising:

::: {.checklist}
- transforms the noisy image;
- thresholds detail coefficients;
- reconstructs the image.
:::

## Denoising Comparison

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week11-wavelet-denoising.png){fig-alt="Original, noisy, Gaussian-smoothed, and wavelet-denoised images"}
:::

## Threshold Strength

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week11-threshold-sweep.png){fig-alt="Wavelet denoising with several threshold strengths"}
:::

## Choosing the Threshold

If the threshold is too small:

::: {.checklist}
- noise remains;
- detail is preserved.
:::

If the threshold is too large:

::: {.checklist}
- noise is removed;
- real texture and small edges may disappear.
:::

## Wavelets Versus TV

| Model | Prior | Typical strength | Typical artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| TV | sparse gradients | sharp edges | staircasing |
| wavelets | sparse multiscale coefficients | localized detail | ringing or texture loss |

::: {.caption}
Both are sparse priors, but they encode different structure.
:::

## What Wavelets Do Not Solve

Wavelets are not automatic truth machines.

::: {.checklist}
- The wavelet family matters.
- Boundary handling matters.
- Texture may not be sparse.
- Thresholds require tuning.
- Inverse problems still need the forward model.
:::

## Code Demo: Run Week 11 Examples {.code-small}

From the repository root:

```bash
python3 examples/week11_wavelets.py
python3 examples/make_week11_figures.py
python3 scripts/build_notebooks.py
./scripts/quarto render
```

## In-Class Notebook Activity

::: {.time-tag}
10 minutes
:::

::: {.exercise-box}
Open the Week 11 notebook.

1. Change the wavelet family.
2. Change the number of decomposition levels.
3. Keep different fractions of coefficients.
4. Change the denoising threshold.
5. Compare wavelet denoising with Gaussian smoothing.
:::

## Quiz-Style Check

::: {.exercise-box}
Answer quickly:

1. What makes wavelets different from Fourier modes?
2. What is a detail coefficient?
3. Why can wavelet coefficients be sparse for images?
4. What does thresholding do?
:::

## Suggested Answers

| Question | Short answer |
|---|---|
| Fourier vs wavelet | global frequency vs localized multiscale pattern |
| detail coefficient | local difference at a scale and orientation |
| sparsity | smooth regions give small details; edges give few large details |
| thresholding | removes or shrinks small coefficients |

## What Students Should Remember

::: {.takeaway-box}
- Wavelets represent images across position, scale, and direction.
- Wavelet transforms split images into coarse approximation and detail subbands.
- Many images are approximately sparse in wavelet coefficients.
- Thresholding gives compression and denoising algorithms.
- Representation choice controls the success of sparse reconstruction.
:::

## Next Time

Model-based versus data-driven imaging:

- handcrafted priors;
- learned priors;
- neural networks as parameterized operators;
- strengths and risks of each viewpoint.
